title: Micron-sized magnonic 3-port rectilinear circulator
authors: Kevin S. Weber, Loic Temdie, Vincent Castel, Timmy Reimann, Morris Lindner, Carsten Dubs, Yves Henry, Matthieu Bailleul, Vincent Vlaminck
abstract: The development of miniaturized non-reciprocal microwave technologies compatible with integrated circuit architectures remains a critical challenge for modern information technology. Here, we present the first experimental characterization of a micron-sized prototypical magnon circulator. Taking advantage of the chiral excitation of spin-waves via nanowire gratings, we propose an original design of a circulator involving three channels of rectilinear and unidirectional spin-wave beams. We demonstrate via a full 3-port spin-wave spectroscopy a genuine spin-wave circulation between the three ports. The narrow frequency band of operation can be tuned over a broad range of frequencies ($2$-$8$ GHz) with both an external field of up to $100$ mT, and the dimensions of the grating specifying the wavevectors. This proposed scheme opens up possibilities for new architectures of integrated and miniaturized non-reciprocal microwave devices.
summary: This paper reports the experimental realization and characterization of a micron-sized magnon circulator using spin-waves in YIG thin films. By exploiting chiral excitation via nanowire gratings, they demonstrated robust non-reciprocal signal routing between three ports. This work advances integrated magnonics for next-generation microwave devices.
main_problem: The development of miniaturized non-reciprocal microwave technologies compatible with integrated circuit architectures is critical for modern information technology.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated genuine spin-wave circulation in a micron-sized magnon circulator, confirming the feasibility of such devices for integrated magnonic circuits.
method: Characterization was performed using 3-port spin-wave spectroscopy with a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA), measuring scattering parameters and extracting dynamic inductance spectra.
model_or_system: The system is a prototype magnon circulator fabricated on a thin Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) film, utilizing chiral excitation of spin-waves via nanowire gratings to achieve non-reciprocity.
key_observables: Spin-wave circulation ($\Delta S_{ij}$ asymmetry), group velocity ($v_g$), attenuation length ($	ext{L}_{	ext{att}}$), and frequency/field dependence of scattering parameters.
important_parameters: Operating frequencies (2–8 GHz), external magnetic fields ($\pm 100$ mT), grating dimensions, and wavevectors ($k_n$).
main_assumptions: The non-reciprocity is governed by the chiral excitation mechanism enabled by the nanowire gratings.
figures_summary: Figures show setup schematics, SEM images of prototypes, frequency-field maps confirming circulation asymmetry, and plots detailing field dependence of extracted spin-wave properties like $v_g$ and $	ext{L}_{	ext{att}}$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the need for miniaturized non-reciprocal devices to presenting the physical mechanism (chiral excitation), followed by detailed experimental characterization across varying fields and frequencies, culminating in analysis of extracted material properties.
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state microwave physics, the concept of engineered wave propagation with inherent directionality and tunable dispersion relations shares mathematical parallels with controlled energy flow in quantum optical cavities or waveguide arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) 3-port spin-wave spectroscopy setup. (inset) Optical microscope image of the circulator prototype. (b) SEM image of the circulator. Arrows indicate the rectilin- ear spin-wave channels and circulation direction defined by the external field polarity. (c) Schematic of the chiral phase- matching mechanism between the microwave field distribution of the gold nanowires (yellow circles) and the spin-wave phase profiles (blue and red circles). (d) Fourier transform of the excitation field from the grating, showing a comb of wavevec- tor kn = n·2π/a. (inset) High-magnification SEM of the gold nano-bar transducers.

FIG. 2. (a) Frequency-field mappings of the relative scattering parameters ∆Sij for the three circulation channels. The k1 branch shows clear amplitude asymmetry consistent with chiral excitation. (b) Complementary (f, Hext) maps ∆Sji. (c) (color) Representative transmission spectra ∆Re(Sij) for i, j ∈{1, 2, 3} at µ0Hext = 40 mT, and their magnitude (grey). All data were acquired at an input power of −15 dBm using a reference field of µ0Href = 200 mT.

FIG. 3. (a) Bias field dependence of the spin-waves transport properties derived from the inductances ∆Lij (extracted from the data in Fig. 2) in the passing polarity of the bias field. (top) group velocity vg, where dashed lines represent theoretical calculations based on the dispersion relation Eq.(2), (middle) attenuation length Latt, and (bottom) relaxation time τr= Latt

FIG. 4. 2nd device with reduced nano-bar width w = 100 nm and periodicity a = 400 nm. (a) Fourier transform of the exci- tation with an inset displaying an SEM image of the reduced nano-bar array. (b) Frequency-field map of ∆S31 (acquired using µ0Href = 250 mT and −5 dBm input power) revealing a second chiral wavevector branch k2. (c) Corresponding spec- tra at 10 mT showing unidirectional transmission for both k1 and k2 peaks, (top) real part Re(S31), and (bottom) magni- tude |S31|. (d) Field dependence of the group velocity vg and the time of flight τToF for both k1 and k2 peak.
FIG. 5. (a) Raw data with insertion and rejection loss. (b) Power dependence at µ0Hext = {100 mT, 200 mT} at µ0Href = 300 mT.
